sources.writer_profile: Omar Nomades is the pen name behind a French-language travel blog focused on Morocco, publishing itineraries and local food guides for Francophone visitors. The site targets independent travellers seeking off-the-beaten-path experiences in Moroccan cities and villages. The Chefchaouen piece was published in April 2025.

Sir Driver Tours is an English-language tour operator blog written anonymously by a tour guide, publishing destination guides and restaurant roundups for international visitors to Morocco. The site covers practical logistics alongside food recommendations. The Chefchaouen restaurant article was published in June 2025.

Chauen.info is an English-language local-oriented guide to Chefchaouen, written anonymously by someone who has lived in the city for ten years. The site publishes practical advice aimed at long-stay visitors and expats alongside general tourists, giving it a more grounded local perspective than typical travel blogs. The restaurant guide was last updated in January 2026.

Emily is the documented first-name persona behind Rediscovering Emily, an English-language personal travel blog chronicling solo travel across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. Her writing combines practical travel tips with personal narrative. The Chefchaouen travel guide was published in October 2025.

@girlwiththepassport is the pseudonym of a Morocco-resident English-language blogger who publishes detailed first-person restaurant reviews and travel guides on Travel Morocco Today. Writing from the perspective of a long-term resident rather than a passing tourist, the blog provides granular price detail and repeat-visit observations. The Chefchaouen restaurant article was published in March 2025.

restaurants.name: Bab Ssour
restaurants.signature_dish: Harira soup (7 MAD), chickpeas in cinnamon sauce, bissara, couscous (Fridays only)
restaurants.quote: If there is one place you have to try authentic Moroccan food, it's Bab Ssour — a restaurant with two floors that is unassuming when you enter, but it's popular for a reason.

restaurants.name: El Cielo
restaurants.signature_dish: Mezze platter (110 MAD) with falafel, hummus, baba ganoush, tabbuli, lebneh, fattoush, tahini
restaurants.quote: It feels like you're entering this fantastic garden oasis filled with culinary delights — bamboo garden with tranquil ponds.

restaurants.name: Cafe Clock
restaurants.signature_dish: Berber Omelet, camel burger, spiced cappuccino (25 MAD)
restaurants.quote: Eating here is one of the reasons I keep visiting Chefchaouen — I always order the Berber Omelet and a spiced cappuccino with cocoa powder and cinnamon for 25 Dirhams.

restaurants.name: Assaada
restaurants.quote: The couscous was cooked pretty well and came with lots of veggies — great value at 40 Dirhams for a plate and a drink.

restaurants.name: Sofia
restaurants.quote: Sofia's quite literally served me the best vegetable tagine of my life. It was the single best thing I had in Morocco, period.

restaurants.name: Lecheria Venezia
restaurants.signature_dish: Moroccan breakfast: harsha, msemen, amlou, honey, jam, cheese, orange juice, mint tea (35 MAD)
restaurants.quote: I really liked the harsha and msemen — generous portions of fresh harsha, msemen, amlou, honey, jam, cheese, orange juice, and mint tea for 35 Dirhams.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Jbaria
restaurants.quote: I was really impressed by the quality of the bread at Jbaria — the bissara comes in quite large portions at just 15 MAD.

restaurants.name: Restaurant DarCom
restaurants.signature_dish: Vegetable couscous with caramelized onions and peanuts
restaurants.quote: The vegetable couscous came with sweet, caramelized onions and peanuts — the bright blue walls and brick archways make it feel like a Moroccan palace.

restaurants.name: La Fuente Restaurant
restaurants.quote: I am really glad I did eat here — the soup quality surprised me and it's very local with informal atmosphere despite being on the main square.

restaurants.name: Chez Hicham
restaurants.quote: Fast and friendly service which is rare when dining at a restaurant right on the main square — the chicken skewers are popular with tour groups.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Lala Mesouda
restaurants.signature_dish: Zalouk (eggplant with tomatoes), Sferjal (lamb tagine with quinoa)
restaurants.quote: Eating at Lala Mesouda is like enjoying some delicious Moroccan home cooking — the Zalouk is a mouthwatering eggplant and tomato dish I keep coming back for.

restaurants.name: Molino Garden
restaurants.signature_dish: Mezze platter (110 MAD), lamb kefta plate (115 MAD)
restaurants.quote: Produce comes from local gardens in the Rif valley. The garden atmosphere provides a refuge from the medina's hustle.

restaurants.name: Morisco
restaurants.signature_dish: Ftour Beldi (traditional breakfast with eggs, local goat cheese, olives, honey, bread)
restaurants.quote: Morisco serves the best Ftour Beldi on the plaza: rural breakfast with eggs, local goat cheese, olives, honey, and bread.

restaurants.name: Casa Aladdin
restaurants.quote: Aladdin attracts with its Game of Thrones style decoration with brass lamps and carpets — visual drama that enhances the dining experience.

restaurants.name: Bilmos
restaurants.quote: Bilmos is the only restaurant verified with real protocols against cross-contamination. The gluten-free menu is physically separated.

restaurants.name: Hamsa
restaurants.quote: Hamsa offers homemade family recipes with clearly identified vegetarian options. The Shakshuka can be prepared without eggs.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Triana
restaurants.quote: Restaurant Triana offers a meal with a view and live music, blending Moroccan and Spanish dishes, named after Seville's Triana district.
